---
title: "Ch 8 â€” Quadrilaterals"
subtitle: "Parallelograms, the midpoint theorem, and the four-cornered family"
lesson-id: "cbse9.quadrilaterals"
tier: "cbse9"
ncert-class: 9
ncert-chapter: 8
ncert-chapter-title: "Quadrilaterals"
board-exam: false
prerequisites: ["cbse8.understanding-quadrilaterals", "cbse9.triangles"]
unlocks: ["cbse9.circles", "cbse10.triangles-similarity"]
applied-domains: ["game-dev", "research"]
estimated-minutes: 45
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

Be honest before you begin.

1. Can you recognise a square, rectangle, rhombus, parallelogram, and
   trapezium? *(If no, revisit
   [cbse8.understanding-quadrilaterals](../../tier-cbse-08-class-8/03-understanding-quadrilaterals/index.qmd).)*
2. Can you write a proof using SSS, SAS, ASA, AAS? *(If no, revisit
   [cbse9.triangles](../07-triangles/index.qmd).)*
3. Can you state that the angles of a triangle sum to $180^\circ$?

Three yes? Read on.
:::

## Touch

Take four straws or pencils. Make them into a four-sided closed figure
with the four corners pinned but able to rotate (think of a hinged
parallelogram-toy). Push gently on a corner. The shape **shears**: it
goes from rectangle to leaning parallelogram, but the opposite sides
stay parallel.

```{ojs}
viewof shear = Inputs.range([-50, 50], {value: 20, step: 1, label: "shear (px)"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const w = 180, h = 90, ox = 60, oy = 60;
  const x1 = ox + shear, y1 = oy;
  const x2 = ox + w + shear, y2 = oy;
  const x3 = ox + w, y3 = oy + h;
  const x4 = ox, y4 = oy + h;
  return html`<svg width="320" height="200" style="border:1px solid #ccc">
    <polygon points="${x1},${y1} ${x2},${y2} ${x3},${y3} ${x4},${y4}"
             fill="rgba(180,200,220,0.4)" stroke="#333" stroke-width="2"/>
    <text x="${x1-12}" y="${y1-6}" font-size="13">A</text>
    <text x="${x2+4}" y="${y2-6}" font-size="13">B</text>
    <text x="${x3+4}" y="${y3+14}" font-size="13">C</text>
    <text x="${x4-14}" y="${y4+14}" font-size="13">D</text>
  </svg>`;
}
```

Drag the slider. Top side $AB$ stays parallel to bottom $DC$, and the
two slanted sides $AD$ and $BC$ stay parallel too. That's a
**parallelogram**. The two diagonals (you can imagine them) always cross
at the same midpoint, even as you shear.

## Wonder

1. The opposite sides of a parallelogram look equal as you shear. Are
   they actually equal â€” for any tilt?
2. Mark the centre $O$ of the parallelogram. Do the two diagonals always
   pass through $O$? Do they cut each other in half?
3. The square, the rhombus, and the rectangle all look like
   parallelograms in disguise. Are they?
4. Take any four-cornered figure (not necessarily nice) and join the
   midpoints of its four sides in order. What do you see?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## A whisper, no answer yet

Q4 is a stunning observation that we will name as the **midpoint
theorem** (and its quadrilateral consequence, **Varignon's theorem** â€”
the midpoints always form a parallelogram).
:::

## Discover

### Problem 1 (Angle sum)

The diagonals of a quadrilateral $ABCD$ are drawn, splitting it into two
triangles, $\triangle ABC$ and $\triangle ACD$. Use the triangle
angle-sum theorem to prove that the four angles of $ABCD$ sum to
$360^\circ$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 â€” a nudge</summary>
Each triangle contributes $180^\circ$. The two triangles share a
diagonal but split the four corners cleanly.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>

| Statement | Reason |
|:--|:--|
| 1. Diagonal $AC$ splits $ABCD$ into $\triangle ABC$ and $\triangle ACD$. | Construction. |
| 2. $\angle BAC + \angle ABC + \angle BCA = 180^\circ$. | Triangle angle sum. |
| 3. $\angle CAD + \angle ACD + \angle ADC = 180^\circ$. | Triangle angle sum. |
| 4. Adding: $(\angle BAC + \angle CAD) + \angle ABC + (\angle BCA + \angle ACD) + \angle ADC = 360^\circ$. |  |
| 5. The grouped sums are $\angle A$ and $\angle C$ of the quadrilateral. So $\angle A + \angle B + \angle C + \angle D = 360^\circ$. | $\blacksquare$ |

</details>

### Problem 2 (Opposite sides of a parallelogram)

In parallelogram $ABCD$, draw diagonal $AC$. Show $\triangle ABC \cong
\triangle CDA$, then deduce $AB = DC$ and $BC = AD$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 â€” a nudge</summary>
Two pairs of parallel sides give two pairs of alternate-interior
angles.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>

| Statement | Reason |
|:--|:--|
| 1. $AB \parallel DC$ (parallelogram). | Definition. |
| 2. $\angle BAC = \angle DCA$. | Alternate interior, transversal $AC$. |
| 3. $AD \parallel BC$ (parallelogram). | Definition. |
| 4. $\angle BCA = \angle DAC$. | Alternate interior, transversal $AC$. |
| 5. $AC = AC$. | Common. |
| 6. $\triangle ABC \cong \triangle CDA$. | ASA. |
| 7. $AB = CD$ and $BC = AD$. | CPCT. $\blacksquare$ |

**Opposite sides of a parallelogram are equal.**
</details>

### Problem 3 (Diagonals bisect each other)

In parallelogram $ABCD$, the diagonals $AC$ and $BD$ meet at $O$. Prove
$O$ bisects each diagonal.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 â€” a nudge</summary>
Compare $\triangle AOB$ and $\triangle COD$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>

| Statement | Reason |
|:--|:--|
| 1. $AB = CD$. | Opposite sides of parallelogram. |
| 2. $\angle OAB = \angle OCD$. | Alternate interior, $AB \parallel DC$ with transversal $AC$. |
| 3. $\angle OBA = \angle ODC$. | Alternate interior, $AB \parallel DC$ with transversal $BD$. |
| 4. $\triangle AOB \cong \triangle COD$. | ASA. |
| 5. $OA = OC$ and $OB = OD$. | CPCT. $\blacksquare$ |

</details>

### Problem 4 (Converse: a test for a parallelogram)

In quadrilateral $ABCD$, both pairs of opposite sides are equal:
$AB = DC$ and $AD = BC$. Prove $ABCD$ is a parallelogram.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 â€” a nudge</summary>
Draw diagonal $AC$ and use SSS.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>

| Statement | Reason |
|:--|:--|
| 1. $AB = CD$, $AD = BC$. | Given. |
| 2. $AC = AC$. | Common. |
| 3. $\triangle ABC \cong \triangle CDA$. | SSS. |
| 4. $\angle BAC = \angle DCA$. | CPCT. |
| 5. So $AB \parallel DC$. | Alternate interior angles equal $\Rightarrow$ lines parallel. |
| 6. Similarly, $\angle BCA = \angle DAC$, so $BC \parallel AD$. | (CPCT and alternate-interior converse.) |
| 7. Both pairs parallel â€” $ABCD$ is a parallelogram. | $\blacksquare$ |

</details>

### Problem 5 (Midpoint theorem)

In $\triangle ABC$, $D$ is the midpoint of $AB$ and $E$ is the midpoint
of $AC$. Prove that $DE \parallel BC$ and $DE = \tfrac{1}{2} BC$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 â€” a nudge</summary>
Extend $DE$ to a point $F$ such that $DE = EF$. Connect $CF$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 â€” the technique</summary>
Show $\triangle AED \cong \triangle CEF$ (SAS). Conclude $CF = AD = DB$
and $CF \parallel AB$. Hence $DBCF$ is a parallelogram.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>

| Statement | Reason |
|:--|:--|
| 1. Extend $DE$ to $F$ with $DE = EF$. | Construction. |
| 2. In $\triangle AED$ and $\triangle CEF$: $AE = CE$ (given), $\angle AED = \angle CEF$ (vert. opp.), $DE = EF$ (construction). | |
| 3. $\triangle AED \cong \triangle CEF$. | SAS. |
| 4. $AD = CF$ and $\angle DAE = \angle FCE$. | CPCT. |
| 5. $AD = DB$ (since $D$ is midpoint of $AB$). So $CF = DB$. | |
| 6. From 4, $AD \parallel CF$, i.e. $AB \parallel CF$. | Alternate interior angles equal. |
| 7. $DBCF$ has one pair of opposite sides ($DB$ and $CF$) both equal and parallel. So $DBCF$ is a parallelogram. | (Test for parallelogram.) |
| 8. So $DF \parallel BC$ and $DF = BC$. | Opposite sides of parallelogram. |
| 9. $DE = \tfrac{1}{2} DF = \tfrac{1}{2} BC$, and $DE \parallel BC$. | $\blacksquare$ |

**The line joining the midpoints of two sides of a triangle is parallel
to the third side and half its length.**
</details>

You discovered: the four angles of any quadrilateral sum to $360^\circ$;
in a parallelogram, opposite sides are equal, opposite angles are equal,
and the diagonals bisect each other; conversely, any of these is enough
to identify a parallelogram; and the midpoint theorem reaches further
into any triangle.

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

A **quadrilateral** is a closed figure with four sides. Its four
interior angles sum to $360^\circ$.

A **parallelogram** is a quadrilateral with both pairs of opposite sides
parallel. The following are equivalent (any one of them implies the
others):

1. Both pairs of opposite sides are parallel.
2. Both pairs of opposite sides are equal.
3. Both pairs of opposite angles are equal.
4. The diagonals bisect each other.
5. One pair of opposite sides is both equal *and* parallel.

Special parallelograms:

- **Rectangle** â€” parallelogram with one right angle (hence all four).
- **Rhombus** â€” parallelogram with adjacent sides equal (hence all four).
- **Square** â€” both rectangle and rhombus.

A **trapezium** is a quadrilateral with one pair of parallel sides.

**Midpoint theorem.** The line joining the midpoints of two sides of a
triangle is parallel to the third side and half as long.

**Converse of the midpoint theorem.** A line drawn through the midpoint
of one side of a triangle, parallel to another side, bisects the third
side.

:::

A short verse:

> *Four corners total a turn, no matter the shape.*
> *Parallel pairs make sides equal, angles equal,*
> *Diagonals halve each other through one centre.*
> *Bisect two sides â€” the join is half the third, and parallel.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> In parallelogram $ABCD$, $E$ is the midpoint of side $BC$. The line
> through $E$ parallel to $AB$ meets $AD$ at $F$. Prove $F$ is the
> midpoint of $AD$.

A competent solver:

1. Draw the figure. $ABCD$ is a parallelogram, so $AB \parallel CD$ and
   $AB = CD$. $E$ is the midpoint of $BC$.
2. **Construct the diagonal** $BD$. Look at $\triangle BCD$: $E$ is the
   midpoint of $BC$. The line through $E$ parallel to $CD$ (and hence
   parallel to $AB$) crosses $BD$ at its midpoint, by the converse of
   the midpoint theorem. Call that midpoint $M$.
3. In $\triangle ABD$: $M$ is the midpoint of $BD$. The line through $M$
   parallel to $AB$ crosses $AD$ at its midpoint, by the converse of
   the midpoint theorem. That intersection is $F$.
4. So $F$ bisects $AD$. $\blacksquare$

### Practice â€” Warm

**W1.** The angles of a quadrilateral are $x$, $2x$, $3x$, $4x$. Find
each.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$10x = 360^\circ \Rightarrow x = 36^\circ$. Angles: $36^\circ, 72^\circ,
108^\circ, 144^\circ$.
</details>

**W2.** A parallelogram has one angle $70^\circ$. Find the other three.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 â€” a nudge</summary>
Opposite angles equal; consecutive angles supplementary.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Two angles are $70^\circ$ and two are $110^\circ$.
</details>

**W3.** Sides of a parallelogram are in the ratio $3:4$ and the
perimeter is $42$. Find the sides.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$2(3k + 4k) = 42 \Rightarrow 14k = 42 \Rightarrow k = 3$. Sides $9$
and $12$.
</details>

### Practice â€” Working

**P1.** In parallelogram $ABCD$, $\angle A = (3x)^\circ$ and
$\angle B = (2x + 25)^\circ$. Find $\angle A$ and $\angle C$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 â€” a nudge</summary>
Adjacent angles of a parallelogram are supplementary.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$3x + 2x + 25 = 180 \Rightarrow 5x = 155 \Rightarrow x = 31$.
$\angle A = 93^\circ$. $\angle C = \angle A = 93^\circ$.
</details>

**P2.** Show that the diagonals of a rhombus are perpendicular to each
other.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 â€” a nudge</summary>
A rhombus is a parallelogram with all sides equal. Diagonals already
bisect each other; use SSS or SAS on the four small triangles.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>

| Statement | Reason |
|:--|:--|
| 1. $ABCD$ rhombus, diagonals meet at $O$. | Given. |
| 2. $OA = OC$, $OB = OD$. | Diagonals of parallelogram bisect each other. |
| 3. $AB = AD$. | All sides of rhombus equal. |
| 4. In $\triangle OAB$ and $\triangle OAD$: $OA = OA$, $OB = OD$, $AB = AD$. | |
| 5. $\triangle OAB \cong \triangle OAD$. | SSS. |
| 6. $\angle AOB = \angle AOD$. | CPCT. |
| 7. $\angle AOB + \angle AOD = 180^\circ$. | Linear pair. |
| 8. So $\angle AOB = 90^\circ$. | $\blacksquare$ |

</details>

**P3.** $ABCD$ is a quadrilateral. $P, Q, R, S$ are midpoints of
$AB, BC, CD, DA$ respectively. Prove $PQRS$ is a parallelogram.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 â€” a nudge</summary>
Draw diagonal $AC$ of $ABCD$. Use the midpoint theorem on $\triangle ABC$
and on $\triangle ACD$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>

| Statement | Reason |
|:--|:--|
| 1. In $\triangle ABC$, $P, Q$ are midpoints of $AB, BC$. | Given. |
| 2. $PQ \parallel AC$ and $PQ = \tfrac{1}{2} AC$. | Midpoint theorem. |
| 3. In $\triangle ACD$, $S, R$ are midpoints of $AD, CD$. | Given. |
| 4. $SR \parallel AC$ and $SR = \tfrac{1}{2} AC$. | Midpoint theorem. |
| 5. So $PQ \parallel SR$ and $PQ = SR$. | From 2, 4. |
| 6. $PQRS$ is a parallelogram. | One pair of opposite sides equal and parallel. $\blacksquare$ |

**This is Varignon's theorem.**
</details>

### Practice â€” Stretch

**S1.** In a parallelogram $ABCD$, $E$ and $F$ are midpoints of $AB$ and
$CD$. Prove that $AECF$ is a parallelogram.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 â€” a nudge</summary>
$AE$ and $CF$ are halves of $AB$ and $CD$. Use the test "one pair of
opposite sides equal and parallel".
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$AE = \tfrac{1}{2} AB$, $CF = \tfrac{1}{2} CD$, and $AB = CD$ (parallelogram).
So $AE = CF$. Also $AE \parallel CF$ since both lie along $AB$ and $CD$
which are parallel. One pair of opposite sides equal and parallel âźą
$AECF$ is a parallelogram. $\blacksquare$
</details>

**S2.** Show that the angle bisectors of a parallelogram form a
rectangle.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 â€” a nudge</summary>
Adjacent angles of a parallelogram are supplementary, so half of each
sum to $90^\circ$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 â€” the technique</summary>
Look at the angle made by two adjacent bisectors at the corner where
they meet inside the parallelogram.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Let bisectors of $\angle A$ and $\angle B$ meet at $P$ inside the
parallelogram. In $\triangle APB$,
$\angle PAB + \angle PBA = \tfrac{1}{2}(\angle A + \angle B) =
\tfrac{1}{2}(180^\circ) = 90^\circ$.
So $\angle APB = 90^\circ$. The same argument at every corner shows the
quadrilateral formed by the four bisectors has four right angles â€”
hence a rectangle. $\blacksquare$
</details>

### Board-Pattern Practice

**1-mark (objective).** A quadrilateral whose diagonals bisect each
other at right angles is:
(a) parallelogram (b) rectangle (c) rhombus (d) trapezium.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**(c) rhombus.** Diagonals of a rhombus bisect at right angles. (A
square also fits, but rhombus is the most general such answer.)
</details>

**2-mark (short answer).** In a parallelogram $ABCD$, $\angle A = 65^\circ$.
Find $\angle B, \angle C, \angle D$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\angle B = 180 - 65 = 115^\circ$. $\angle C = 65^\circ$. $\angle D =
115^\circ$.
</details>

**3-mark (short answer II).** $ABCD$ is a parallelogram. Prove that
the diagonal $AC$ divides it into two congruent triangles.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 â€” a nudge</summary>
This is Discover Problem 2.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Reproduce the four-row two-column proof of Discover Problem 2 with ASA.
$\blacksquare$
</details>

**5-mark (long answer).** Prove the **midpoint theorem**: in
$\triangle ABC$, the line joining the midpoints of $AB$ and $AC$ is
parallel to $BC$ and half its length.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 â€” a nudge</summary>
Reproduce Discover Problem 5.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
See Discover Problem 5 above. Construct $F$ on the extension of $DE$
with $DE = EF$. Show $\triangle AED \cong \triangle CEF$ by SAS, deduce
$DBCF$ is a parallelogram, and conclude $DE = \tfrac{1}{2} BC$ and
$DE \parallel BC$. $\blacksquare$
</details>

::: {.callout-note}
**NCERT Exemplar pointer:** Class 9 Exemplar Ch. 8, Problem 8.4
(midpoints of a quadrilateral form a parallelogram, similar to P3).
**Previous-year pointer:** CBSE Class 9 sample paper "prove midpoint
theorem" (similar to 5-mark above).
:::

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"All parallelograms are rectangles."** No â€” only those with right
   angles. Rectangles, rhombi, and squares are *special* parallelograms.
2. **"A trapezium has both pairs of opposite sides parallel."** That
   would make it a parallelogram. A trapezium has *exactly one* pair
   parallel. (American usage flips this term â€” "trapezoid" â€” but the
   NCERT convention follows British: trapezium = one pair parallel.)
3. **"The midpoint theorem requires the triangle to be right-angled."**
   It works for **every** triangle.
:::

### Self-Assessment

Be honest with yourself.

1. Could you prove "diagonals of a parallelogram bisect each other"
   without notes?
2. Did you solve W1â€“P3 with at most one hint each?
3. Could you state the midpoint theorem and use it on a fresh problem
   without prompting?

- **3 yes:** move on to **Circles**.
- **2 yes:** redo S1 and S2.
- **0â€“1 yes:** re-read *Discover* 2 and 5 â€” they are the proof spine
  of this chapter.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- [`cbse8.understanding-quadrilaterals`](../../tier-cbse-08-class-8/03-understanding-quadrilaterals/index.qmd)
  â€” naming, identifying, classifying quadrilaterals.
- [`cbse9.triangles`](../07-triangles/index.qmd) â€” congruence rules,
  CPCT, isosceles theorem.

### What this unlocks

- [`cbse9.circles`](../09-circles/index.qmd) â€” cyclic-quadrilateral
  results lean directly on the parallelogram and angle-sum work here.
- [`cbse10.triangles-similarity`](../../tier-cbse-10-class-10/06-triangles-similarity/index.qmd)
  â€” proportionality theorem (basic proportionality / Thales) is the
  midpoint theorem rescaled.

### NCERT chapter alignment

- **Class 9, Chapter 8 â€” Quadrilaterals** (NCERT 2023 rationalised
  syllabus). Covers Sections 8.1â€“8.2: properties of parallelograms,
  midpoint theorem.
- **Sister synthesis lesson** (different angle):
  [`t1.shapes-angles`](../../tier-1-foundations/12-shapes-angles/index.qmd)
  introduces the family at a concrete level. The proofs are this
  chapter's contribution.
- **Read alongside the NCERT textbook**.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Game development.** A 2D physics engine treats a flat object as a
quadrilateral. Detecting whether two parallelograms overlap (used for
hitboxes, dragging, snap-to-grid) reduces to the diagonal-bisection
property â€” find the centre, check rotations.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research.** Varignon's theorem (P3) â€” that the midpoints of any
quadrilateral form a parallelogram â€” is the start of *affine geometry*,
the geometry that survives when you forget about distance and angle but
keep parallelism. CAD systems and computer graphics rely on the
distinction.
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- **Touchstones honoured:** Kiselev (proof discipline), AoPS (Varignon's
  theorem as P3), Russian problem-first (Discover sequence builds the
  parallelogram facts in dependency order).
:::
